conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria to make sure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for reaching out! I can help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have a child. My son Alex is 6 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you some questions.

Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Next question:

Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or the equivalent in kind) towards his care - for example on food, clothes or pocket money?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me, and I'm not currently contributing financially towards his care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask a few more questions to complete the assessment.

Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or living with someone else (such as another parent, relative, or friend)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's been in hospital for a while now, about 3 months.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that. I have a few more questions about Alex's situation.

During the time Alex has been in hospital, have you been regularly spending money on him (for example, on clothes, toys, books, or other items for him)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have. I still buy his clothes, magazines, and his favorite food while he's in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful to know. I have one more important question:

Is Alex in a hospital in the UK, or is he in a hospital abroad?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's in a hospital here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on what you've told me:
- Alex is 6 years old (under 16, so within the age requirement)
- You live in the UK
- Alex doesn't live with you and you're not making financial contributions to his upkeep in the usual sense
- However, Alex has been in a UK hospital for about 3 months (12 weeks)
- You have been regularly spending money on him during his hospital stay (clothes, magazines, and food)

According to the Child Benefit rules, normally after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, you would no longer be entitled to Child Benefit. However, there is an important exception: you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them, even after the 12-week period.

Since you have been regularly spending money on Alex while he's in hospital, you should be eligible for Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news!** Based on the information you've provided, you are **eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Even though Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks and doesn't live with you, you qualify under an important exception: because Alex is in a UK hospital and you regularly spend money on him (buying his clothes, magazines, and favorite food), you can continue to receive Child Benefit for him.

I hope Alex recovers well and returns home soon. If you have any other questions about Child Benefit or if your circumstances change, please don't hesitate to reach out.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old (under 16). The claimant lives in the UK. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (over the 12-week threshold), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food). According to the rules, for children in a UK hospital, if the claimant regularly spends money on them, Child Benefit can continue even after 12 weeks. Therefore, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 6-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for over 12 weeks, the claimant qualifies under the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child is in a UK hospital and the claimant regularly spends money on them.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 6 years old (under 16). The claimant lives in the UK. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (over the 12-week threshold), the claimant regularly spends money on Alex (buying clothes, magazines, and food). According to the rules, for children in a UK hospital, if the claimant regularly spends money on them, Child Benefit can continue even after 12 weeks. Therefore, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 6-year-old son Alex. Despite Alex being in hospital for over 12 weeks, the claimant qualifies under the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child is in a UK hospital and the claimant regularly spends money on them.
